title: php
transcript:
[00:00] PHP, a dynamic interpreted scripting language for building interactive websites on the server.
[00:06] Despite the haters pronouncing it dead, it remains one of the most popular languages for backend web development.
[00:11] It powers content management systems like WordPress, top-tier websites like Wikipedia, and countless others via frameworks like Laravel and Symfony.
[00:18] Hell, man, even Facebook uses it, although they built a custom compiler to convert it to machine code on their servers.
[00:24] It was created in 1994 by Rasmus Lierdorf to manage his personal homepage.
[00:28] Clever acronym, but it sounds lame for a language that powers multi-billion dollar enterprises.
[00:33] So today we practice cognitive dissonance to tell ourselves PHP stands for Hypertext Preprocessor.
[00:39] It has a special place in history because it predates JavaScript and was one of the first languages to be embedded directly in HTML,
[00:45] allowing websites to be built dynamically on a server.
[00:48] It's open source and was one of the technologies that revolutionized the web by making application development accessible to the average person.
[00:55] Its interpreter is implemented in C and has a syntax inspired by Perl.
[00:59] To get started, create a PHP file and add some basic HTML to it.
[01:04] Within this file you can go into PHP mode by opening a tag The code within these tags will be rendered on the server Use echo to output a value and the location of the tag To declare a variable
[01:16] give it a name that starts with a dollar sign followed by a value. It's a weekly type language,
[01:21] so no type annotations are necessary. It also comes with a large number of predefined variables
[01:25] geared towards web development, like get, post, and cookie, which contain information about the
[01:30] incoming HTTP request. Now when this code is rendered on your server, it will output an HTML
[01:35] string with the PHP tags being replaced by whatever you echoed. The language also supports multiple
[01:41] programming paradigms. You have first class functions, which can be assigned to variables
[01:45] or used anonymously as arguments. And it has tons and tons of built in functions to do pretty much
[01:50] anything a web developer could ever imagine doing. It also has a complete object model where you can
[01:55] define classes with inheritance to implement object oriented patterns. We're currently on PHP
[02:00] version 8, which has all the features you would expect from a modern pragmatic language. Every
[02:04] web developer should be familiar with it, because statistically, there's a 78.5% chance that your
[02:10] client's website is running the personal homepage language. This has been PHP in 100 seconds. Hit
[02:15] the like button if you want to see more short videos like this. Thanks for watching, and I
[02:18] will see you in the next one.

summary.overview: The lecture provides an introduction to PHP, a dynamic interpreted scripting language used for building interactive websites on the server, highlighting its history, features, and importance in web development. Despite being pronounced dead by some, PHP remains one of the most popular languages for backend web development, powering content management systems and top-tier websites. The lecture aims to provide a brief overview of PHP and its capabilities.
main_points: PHP was created in 1994 by Rasmus Lierdorf
PHP powers content management systems like WordPress and top-tier websites like Wikipedia
PHP has a special place in history as one of the first languages to be embedded directly in HTML
PHP is open source and has a syntax inspired by Perl
PHP supports multiple programming paradigms, including object-oriented programming
key_concepts: PHP: a dynamic interpreted scripting language
Hypertext Preprocessor: a language for building interactive websites on the server
Interpreter: a program that executes PHP code on the server
action_items: Create a PHP file and practice adding basic HTML and PHP code
Review PHP syntax and data types
Explore PHP frameworks like Laravel and Symfony
